q: In a fluorescent lamp choke (a small transformer) <math><mn>100</mn><mi></mi><mi>V</mi></math> of reverse voltage is produced when the choke current changes uniformly from <math><mn>0.25</mn><mi></mi><mi>A</mi></math> to <math><mn>0</mn></math> in a duration of <math><mn>0.025</mn><mi></mi><mi>m</mi><mi>s</mi></math> . The self-inductance of the choke (in <math><mi>m</mi><mi>H</mi></math> ) is estimated to be ________
question: In a fluorescent lamp choke (a small transformer) <math><mn>100</mn><mi></mi><mi>V</mi></math> of reverse voltage is produced when the choke current changes uniformly from <math><mn>0.25</mn><mi></mi><mi>A</mi></math> to <math><mn>0</mn></math> in a duration of <math><mn>0.025</mn><mi></mi><mi>m</mi><mi>s</mi></math> . The self-inductance of the choke (in <math><mi>m</mi><mi>H</mi></math> ) is estimated to be ________
solution:
100=L0.250.025×103
∴L=100×10-4H
=10 mH

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular loop of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, with its sides parallel to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis respectively moves with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> axis direction, in a space containing a variable magnetic field in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> direction. The field has a gradient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> along the negative <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> direction and it is decreasing with time at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the resistance of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi>Ω</mi></math>, the power dissipated by the loop as heat is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A rectangular loop of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, with its sides parallel to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis respectively moves with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> axis direction, in a space containing a variable magnetic field in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> direction. The field has a gradient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> along the negative <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> direction and it is decreasing with time at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the resistance of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi>Ω</mi></math>, the power dissipated by the loop as heat is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Let B0 is the magnetic field at origin. Then,

dBdx=-10-310-2

⇒∫B0BdB=-∫0x10-1dx

⇒B-B0=-10-1x

⇒B=B0-x10

Motional emf in AB=0

Motional emf in CD=0

Motional emf in AD=ε1=B0ℓv

Magnetic field on rod BC 

=B0--12×10-210

Motional emf in  BC=ε2=B0+12×10-210ℓ×v

Net equivalent emf εeq=ε2-ε1=300×10-7 V

For time variation

εeq'=AdBdt=60×10-7 V

εeqnet =εeq+εeq'=360×10-7 V

Power r=εeq net 2R=216×10-9 W

q: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When capacitor with capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math> is put in series, the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
question: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When capacitor with capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math> is put in series, the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
solution:
In case of L-R circuit

Z=XL2+R2 Therefore, power factor

P1=cosϕ=RZ

As XL=R ⇒Z=2R

⇒P1=R2R⇒P1=12

In case of  L-C-R circuit

Z=R2+XL-XC2

According to the question XL=XC ⇒Z=R  ⇒P2=cosϕ=RR=1

⇒P1P2=12

q:
A conducting bar of length L is free to slide on two parallel conducting rails as shown in the figure

Two resistors R1 and R2 are connected across the ends of the rails. There is a uniform magnetic field B→ pointing into the page. An external agent pulls the bar to the left at a constant speed v.

The correct statement about the directions of induced currents I1 and I2 flowing through R1 and R2 respectively is :
question:
A conducting bar of length L is free to slide on two parallel conducting rails as shown in the figure

Two resistors R1 and R2 are connected across the ends of the rails. There is a uniform magnetic field B→ pointing into the page. An external agent pulls the bar to the left at a constant speed v.

The correct statement about the directions of induced currents I1 and I2 flowing through R1 and R2 respectively is :
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are in anticlockwise direction

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are in clockwise direction

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is in clockwise direction and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is in anticlockwise direction

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is in anticlockwise direction and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is in clockwise direction
solution:
Given, the external agent pulls the bar towards left. According to Lenz's law and Fleming's right hand rule, there will be a motional EMF generated in the bar. The right-hand rule tells us that the magnetic force on positive charges will be in downward direction and the electric equivalent circuit can be drawn as shown below.

So, I1 is in the clockwise direction and I2 is in the anticlockwise direction.

subject: Physics
q: The current in an inductor is given by $\mathrm{I}=(3 \mathrm{t}+8)$ where $\mathrm{t}$ is in second. The magnitude of induced emf produced in the inductor is $12 \mathrm{mV}$. The self-inductance of the inductor _____ $\mathrm{mH}$.
question: The current in an inductor is given by $\mathrm{I}=(3 \mathrm{t}+8)$ where $\mathrm{t}$ is in second. The magnitude of induced emf produced in the inductor is $12 \mathrm{mV}$. The self-inductance of the inductor _____ $\mathrm{mH}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}=3 \mathrm{t}+8 \\ & \varepsilon=12 \mathrm{mV} \\ & |\varepsilon|=\mathrm{L}\left|\frac{\mathrm{dI}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right| \\ & 12=\mathrm{L} \times 3 \\ & \mathrm{~L}=4 \mathrm{mH}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A $30$ cm long solenoid has $10$ turns per cm and area of $5$ cm$^2$. The current through the solenoid coil varies from $2$ A to $4$ A in $3.14$ s. The e.m.f. induced in the coil is $\alpha\times 10^{-5}$ V. The value $\alpha$ is ________.
question: A $30$ cm long solenoid has $10$ turns per cm and area of $5$ cm$^2$. The current through the solenoid coil varies from $2$ A to $4$ A in $3.14$ s. The e.m.f. induced in the coil is $\alpha\times 10^{-5}$ V. The value $\alpha$ is ________.
solution:
Given:
Length of the solenoid, $l = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
Number of turns per unit length, $n = 10 \text{ turns/cm} = 1000 \text{ turns/m}$
Area of cross-section, $A = 5 \text{ cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
Change in current, $\Delta I = 4 \text{ A} - 2 \text{ A} = 2 \text{ A}$
Time interval, $\Delta t = 3.14 \text{ s}$

The self-inductance of the solenoid is given by:
$L = \mu_0 n^2 A l$

Substituting the given values:
$L = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times (1000)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3$
$L = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$
$L = 6\pi \times 10^{-5} \text{ H}$

The magnitude of the induced e.m.f. is:
$e = L \dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta t}$

Substituting the values of $L$, $\Delta I$, and $\Delta t$:
$e = 6\pi \times 10^{-5} \times \dfrac{2}{3.14}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:
$e = 6 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 = 12 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}$

Comparing this with $e = \alpha \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}$, we get:
$\alpha = 12$

Answer: $12$

subject: Physics
q: When the current changes from $+2 \mathrm{~A}$ to $-2 \mathrm{~A}$ in $0.05$ second, an e.m.f. of $8 \mathrm{~V}$ is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self-induction of the coil is
question: When the current changes from $+2 \mathrm{~A}$ to $-2 \mathrm{~A}$ in $0.05$ second, an e.m.f. of $8 \mathrm{~V}$ is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self-induction of the coil is
solution: $e=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{dl}}{\mathrm{dt}} \Rightarrow 8=(\mathrm{L}) \frac{2-(-2)}{0.05} \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}=0.1 \mathrm{H}$

subject: Physics
q: The self inductance of the motor of an electric fan is $10 \mathrm{H}$. In order to impart maximum power at $50 \mathrm{~Hz}$, it should be connected to a capacitance of
question: The self inductance of the motor of an electric fan is $10 \mathrm{H}$. In order to impart maximum power at $50 \mathrm{~Hz}$, it should be connected to a capacitance of
solution:
$f=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}}$
$C=\frac{1}{4 \times \pi^2 f^2 \times 10}$
$C=1 \mu F$

subject: Physics
q: In the given figure, an inductor and resistor are connected in series with a battery of emf E volt. Ea2b J s-1 represents the maximum rate at which the energy is stored in the magnetic field (inductor). The numerical value of ba will be _____.
question: In the given figure, an inductor and resistor are connected in series with a battery of emf E volt. Ea2b J s-1 represents the maximum rate at which the energy is stored in the magnetic field (inductor). The numerical value of ba will be _____.
solution:
The formula to calculate the current flowing through an L-R circuit is given by

i=ER1-e-RtL   ...1

The energy stored within the inductor can be calculated as follows:

Einduction =12Li2=12LE2R21-e-RtL2   ...2

The rate of change of energy stored within the inductor is given by

ddtEinduction =21-e-RtL·e-RtL·RL·E2L2R2=E2Re-RtL-e-2RtL   ...3

For maximum rate,

d2Edt2=0   ...4

From equation (3) and (4), it can be written that

ddtE2Re-RtL-e-2RtL =0⇒e-RtL=2e-2RtL  ⇒eRtL=2   ...5

From equation (3) and (5), it can be written, under maximum energy stored condition for the circuit, that

ddtEinduction =E2R2-1-2-2=E24R

Substitute the values of the resistance and compare with the given expression to obtain the values of the parameters.

a=2b=2R=50

Hence, the required ratio is given by

ba=25

q:
The flux linked with a coil at any instant ' $t$ ' is given by
$\phi=10 t^2-50 t+250$
The induced emf at $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$ is
question:
The flux linked with a coil at any instant ' $t$ ' is given by
$\phi=10 t^2-50 t+250$
The induced emf at $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$ is
solution: $e=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-(20 t-50)=-10$ volt

q: A square loop of side $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ being moved towards right at a constant speed of 2 $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$ as shown in figure. The front edge enters the $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ wide magnetic field at $t=$ 0 . The value of induced emf in the loop at $t=10 \mathrm{~s}$ will be :
question: A square loop of side $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ being moved towards right at a constant speed of 2 $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$ as shown in figure. The front edge enters the $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ wide magnetic field at $t=$ 0 . The value of induced emf in the loop at $t=10 \mathrm{~s}$ will be :
solution: At $\mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{sec}$ complete loop is in magnetic field therefore no change in flux $\mathrm{e}=\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=0$ $\mathrm{e}=0$ for complete loop

subject: Physics
q: A conducting circular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.4 T with its plane perpendicular to the field. Somehow, the radius of the loop starts expanding at a constant rate of 1 mm s-1. The magnitude of induced emf in the loop at an instant when the radius of the loop is 2 cm will be _______ μV.
question: A conducting circular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.4 T with its plane perpendicular to the field. Somehow, the radius of the loop starts expanding at a constant rate of 1 mm s-1. The magnitude of induced emf in the loop at an instant when the radius of the loop is 2 cm will be _______ μV.
solution:
The data given is 

B=0.4 TR=2×10-2 m

It is given that dRdt=10-3 m s-1

Area of the loop is A=πR2

So, dAdt=2πRdRdt

The flux is,

ϕ=2πRdRdt×B

So, the magnitude of emf is 

ε=2×227×2×10-2×10-3×0.4

=5.028×10-5 V=50.28 μV≈50  μV

subject: Physics
q: A 1 m long metal rod XY completes the circuit as shown in figure. The plane of the circuit is perpendicular to the magnetic field of flux density 0.15 T. If the resistance of the circuit is 5 Ω, the force needed to move the rod in direction, as indicated, with a constant speed of 4 m s-1 will be _______10–3 N.
question: A 1 m long metal rod XY completes the circuit as shown in figure. The plane of the circuit is perpendicular to the magnetic field of flux density 0.15 T. If the resistance of the circuit is 5 Ω, the force needed to move the rod in direction, as indicated, with a constant speed of 4 m s-1 will be _______10–3 N.
solution:
The force on the rod is given by

F=ilB

⇒F=BvlR×lB

⇒F=B2l2vR   ...(i)

The data given is

R=5 Ωl=1 mv=4 m s-1B=0.15 T

Substituting the values in equation (i)

F=0.152×12×45

=18×10-3 N

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of inductance $10$ mH having resistance of $100\,\Omega$ is connected to battery of E.M.F. $1.0$ V through a switch as shown in the figure below. After switch is closed, the ratio of instantaneous voltages across the inductor when the current passing through it is $2$ mA and $4$ mA is _______.
question: An inductor of inductance $10$ mH having resistance of $100\,\Omega$ is connected to battery of E.M.F. $1.0$ V through a switch as shown in the figure below. After switch is closed, the ratio of instantaneous voltages across the inductor when the current passing through it is $2$ mA and $4$ mA is _______.
solution:
In an LR circuit, the equation for the voltage is given by Kirchhoff's loop law:

$E = L \dfrac{di}{dt} + iR$

The instantaneous voltage across the ideal inductor is the induced EMF, which is given by:

$V_L = L \dfrac{di}{dt} = E - iR$

Given values are:
$E = 1.0$ V
$R = 100\,\Omega$

For current $i_1 = 2$ mA $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ A, the voltage across the inductor is:

$V_{L1} = 1.0 - (2 \times 10^{-3} \times 100) = 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8$ V

For current $i_2 = 4$ mA $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ A, the voltage across the inductor is:

$V_{L2} = 1.0 - (4 \times 10^{-3} \times 100) = 1.0 - 0.4 = 0.6$ V

The ratio of the instantaneous voltages is:

$\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{0.8}{0.6} = \dfrac{4}{3}$

Answer: $4/3$

The potential difference between the two faces of the cube, perpendicular to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math>-axis, is
question: A solid metal cube of edge length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> is moving in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math>-direction, at a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. There is a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>0.1</mtext><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math>-direction. The potential difference between the two faces of the cube, perpendicular to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math>-axis, is
solution:
emf developed is given by, e=Bvl

∴e=0.12×10-2×6⇒e=12×10-3=12 mV

subject: Physics
q: A coil is suspended in a uniform magnetic field, with the plane of the coil parallel to the magnetic lines of force. When a current is passed through the coil it starts oscillating; it is very difficult to stop. But if an aluminium plate is placed near to the coil, it stops. This is due to :
question: A coil is suspended in a uniform magnetic field, with the plane of the coil parallel to the magnetic lines of force. When a current is passed through the coil it starts oscillating; it is very difficult to stop. But if an aluminium plate is placed near to the coil, it stops. This is due to :
options: development of air current when the plate is placed.
induction of electrical charge on the plate
shielding of magnetic lines of force as aluminium is a paramagnetic material.
electromagnetic induction in the aluminium plate giving rise to electromagnetic damping.
solution: Oscillating coil produces time variable magnetic field. It cause eddy current in the aluminium plate which causes anti-torque on the coil, due to which is stops.

subject: Physics
q: Consider an electrical circuit containing a two way switch S. Initially S is open and then T1 is connected to T2. As the current in R=6 Ω attains a maximum value of steady-state level, T1 is disconnected from T2 and immediately connected to T3. Potential drop across r=3 Ω resistor immediately after T1 is connected to T3 is ______V. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)
question: Consider an electrical circuit containing a two way switch S. Initially S is open and then T1 is connected to T2. As the current in R=6 Ω attains a maximum value of steady-state level, T1 is disconnected from T2 and immediately connected to T3. Potential drop across r=3 Ω resistor immediately after T1 is connected to T3 is ______V. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)
solution:
When T1 and T2 are connected, then the steady state current in the inductor I=66=1 A

When T1 and T3 are connected then current through inductor remains same. So potential difference across 3 Ω

V=Ir=1×3=3 V

q: A small square loop of wire of side l is placed inside a large square loop of wire LL≫l. Both loops are coplanar and their centres coincide at point O as shown in figure. The mutual inductance of the system is
question: A small square loop of wire of side l is placed inside a large square loop of wire LL≫l. Both loops are coplanar and their centres coincide at point O as shown in figure. The mutual inductance of the system is
solution:
Let a current, I pass through the square loop of side, L. The magnetic field at the centre O,

B=4×Magnetic field due to each side

⇒B=4×μ04π⋅IL/2sin 45∘+sin 45∘

⇒B=2μ0IπL12+12=22μ0IπL

Magnetic flux linked with the small square loop,

ϕ=BA=Bl2=22μ0Il2πL

Mutual inductance of the coil, 

M=ϕI=22μ0l2πL.

q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long horizontal wire extends from North East to South West. It is falling with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> at right angles to the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of the induced emf in the wire is:
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long horizontal wire extends from North East to South West. It is falling with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> at right angles to the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of the induced emf in the wire is:
solution:
The wire makes an angle 45° with the magnetic field lines. Therefore, the induced emf will be,

ε=Bvlsin450

=0.3×10-4×5×10×12

=1.1×10-3 V

subject: Physics
q: When a coil is connected across a $20 \mathrm{~V}$ dc supply, it draws a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$. When it is connected across $20 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ ac supply, it draws a current of $4 \mathrm{~A}$. The self inductance of the coil is ______ $\mathrm{mH}$. $($ Take $\pi=3$ )
question: When a coil is connected across a $20 \mathrm{~V}$ dc supply, it draws a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$. When it is connected across $20 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ ac supply, it draws a current of $4 \mathrm{~A}$. The self inductance of the coil is ______ $\mathrm{mH}$. $($ Take $\pi=3$ )
solution:
Case-I:
$i=\frac{20}{R} \Rightarrow R=4 \Omega$ Case-II:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{i}=\frac{20}{\mathrm{Z}} \\ & 4=\frac{20}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2}=5 \\ & \mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2=25 \Rightarrow \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=3 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{L}=\frac{3}{2 \pi \mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{2 \times 50}=\frac{1000}{100} \mathrm{mH} \\ & \mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{mH}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of inductance $\mathrm{L}=400 \mathrm{mH}$ and resistors of resistances $\mathrm{R}_1$ $=2 \Omega$ and $R_2=2 \Omega$ are connected to a battery of emf $12 \mathrm{~V}$ as shown in the figure. The internal resistance of the battery is negligible. The switch $S$ is closed at $t=0$. The potential drop across $L$ as a function of time is
question: An inductor of inductance $\mathrm{L}=400 \mathrm{mH}$ and resistors of resistances $\mathrm{R}_1$ $=2 \Omega$ and $R_2=2 \Omega$ are connected to a battery of emf $12 \mathrm{~V}$ as shown in the figure. The internal resistance of the battery is negligible. The switch $S$ is closed at $t=0$. The potential drop across $L$ as a function of time is
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_1=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{R}_1}=\frac{12}{2}=6 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{dl_{2 }}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{R}_2 \times \mathrm{I}_2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_2=\mathrm{I}_0\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{c}}}\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{o}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_2}=\frac{12}{2}=6 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{400 \times 10^{-3}}{2}=0.2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_2=6\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / 0.2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Potential drop across $L=E-R_2 l_2=12-2 \times 6\left(1-e^{-b t}\right)=12 e^{-5 t}$
Directions: Question numbers 28,29 and 30 are based on the following paragraph. Two moles of helium gas are taken over the cycle $A B C D A$, as shown in the $P-T$ diagram.

subject: Physics
q:
A circular loop of radius $20\text{ cm}$ and resistance $2\ \Omega$ is placed in a time varying magnetic field $\vec{B} = (2t^2 + 2t + 3)\text{ T}$. At $t = 0$, for the plane of the loop being perpendicular to the magnetic field and, the induced current in the loop at $t = 3\text{ s}$ is $\dfrac{\alpha}{50}\text{ A}$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
(Take $\pi = 22/7$)
question:
A circular loop of radius $20\text{ cm}$ and resistance $2\ \Omega$ is placed in a time varying magnetic field $\vec{B} = (2t^2 + 2t + 3)\text{ T}$. At $t = 0$, for the plane of the loop being perpendicular to the magnetic field and, the induced current in the loop at $t = 3\text{ s}$ is $\dfrac{\alpha}{50}\text{ A}$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
(Take $\pi = 22/7$)
solution:
The magnetic flux linked with the circular loop is given by $\Phi = BA \cos \theta$. Since the plane of the loop is perpendicular to the magnetic field, the angle between the area vector and the magnetic field is $\theta = 0^{\circ}$.

$\Phi = B \cdot \pi r^2$

Given $r = 20\text{ cm} = 0.2\text{ m}$, the area of the loop is:

$A = \pi (0.2)^2 = 0.04\pi\text{ m}^2$

The induced emf in the loop is given by Faraday's law of induction:

$|E| = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = A \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

Given the magnetic field $B = 2t^2 + 2t + 3$, we differentiate it with respect to time $t$:

$\dfrac{dB}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(2t^2 + 2t + 3) = 4t + 2$

At $t = 3\text{ s}$, the rate of change of the magnetic field is:

$\dfrac{dB}{dt} = 4(3) + 2 = 14\text{ T/s}$

Now, substituting the values into the emf equation:

$|E| = 0.04\pi \times 14 = 0.56\pi\text{ V}$

The induced current $I$ in the loop is:

$I = \dfrac{|E|}{R} = \dfrac{0.56\pi}{2} = 0.28\pi\text{ A}$

Using $\pi = \dfrac{22}{7}$, we get:

$I = 0.28 \times \dfrac{22}{7} = 0.04 \times 22 = 0.88\text{ A}$

We are given that the induced current is $\dfrac{\alpha}{50}\text{ A}$. Equating the two values:

$\dfrac{\alpha}{50} = 0.88$

$\alpha = 0.88 \times 50 = 44$

Answer: $44$

The two loops are in the same plane. The outer loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> carries an alternating current <math><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>I</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> The emf induced in the smaller inner loop is nearly:
question: A small circular loop of wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>  is located at the centre of a much larger circular wire loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math>. The two loops are in the same plane. The outer loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> carries an alternating current <math><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>I</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> The emf induced in the smaller inner loop is nearly:
solution:
Induced emf,

ε=MdIdt,

Current is given as, I=I0cosωt 

M=μ0πa22b, ε=-Mddt (I0cosωt)

c=M ωI0 sinωt

=μ0πa22bω I0sin⁡ ωt

q: A circular coil of 1000 turns each with area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is rotated about its vertical diameter at the rate of one revolution per second in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum voltage generation will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
question: A circular coil of 1000 turns each with area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is rotated about its vertical diameter at the rate of one revolution per second in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum voltage generation will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
solution:
Given, N=1000, A=1 m2 and B=0.07 T

The maximum emf generated is εmax=NBAω,

=NBA2πf

 =1000×0.07×1×2π1=140π

Thus, maximum emf is 439.6 V≈440 V.

q: A metallic cube of side 15 cm moving along y-axis at a uniform velocity of 2 m s-1. In a region of uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.5 T directed along z- axis. In equilibrium the potential difference between the faces of higher and lower potential developed because of the motion through the field will be mV.
question: A metallic cube of side 15 cm moving along y-axis at a uniform velocity of 2 m s-1. In a region of uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.5 T directed along z- axis. In equilibrium the potential difference between the faces of higher and lower potential developed because of the motion through the field will be mV.
solution:
Using the equation

 qE=qvB ⇒E=vB

Using the relation ∆V=Ed

⇒∆V=vBd

=2×12×0.15 V
=0.15 V=150 mV

subject: Physics
q: A constant magnetic field of 1 T is applied in the x>0 region. A metallic circular ring of radius 1 m is moving with a constant velocity of 1 m s-1 along the x-axis. At t=0 s, the centre O of the ring is at x=-1 m. What will be the value of the induced emf in the ring at t=1 s ? (Assume the velocity of the ring does not change.)
question: A constant magnetic field of 1 T is applied in the x>0 region. A metallic circular ring of radius 1 m is moving with a constant velocity of 1 m s-1 along the x-axis. At t=0 s, the centre O of the ring is at x=-1 m. What will be the value of the induced emf in the ring at t=1 s ? (Assume the velocity of the ring does not change.)
solution: Recall the formula of induced E.M.F. in terms of the length of the rod, and uniform magnetic field, E=Bvl, here in the case of the ring, l=2R,⇒E=Bv2R, when half ring enters, E=1×1×2×1=2 V.

q: In the circuit shown below, the key $\mathrm{K}$ is closed at $\mathrm{t}=0$. The current through the battery is
question: In the circuit shown below, the key $\mathrm{K}$ is closed at $\mathrm{t}=0$. The current through the battery is
solution:
At $\mathrm{t}=0$, inductor behaves like an infinite resistance
So at $t=0, i=\frac{V}{R_2}$ and at $t=\infty$, inductor behaves like a conducting wire
$$
\mathrm{i}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\text {eq }}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}\left(\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2\right)}{\mathrm{R}_1 \mathrm{R}_2}
$$

question: A rectangular metallic loop is moving out of a uniform magnetic field region to a field free region with a constant speed. When the loop is partially inside the magnate field, the plot of magnitude of induced emf $(\varepsilon)$ with time $(t)$ is given by
solution: $\begin{aligned} & E=-\frac{d \phi}{d t} \\ & \phi=B b x \\ & |E|=B b v\end{aligned}$
